Secretary Taft transmits Taft Board report to President Roosevelt recommending radical modifications to Endicott plan, including adoption of 14-inch guns as maximum (replacing 12-inch) where highest power required. Total estimated cost for US fortifications materially increased from 1888 estimate.

Henry L. Stimson sworn in as Secretary of War, succeeding Jacob M. Dickinson. Ceremony at War Department office in presence of Senator Root and Representative Dwight. Stimson received all Army officers on duty in Washington and civil officers of the War Department. Background: Yale 1888, Harvard Law 1890, U.S. District Attorney for Southern District of NY 1906-09. Prior military service in Squadron A, N.G.N.Y. for ~9 years; qualified as distinguished expert with carbine.
Crozier on special ordnance duty in Europe; studies recent artillery developments at leading British, French, and German arsenals, shaping nearly every innovation he later introduced into US ordnance

Construction and Modification of Haskell Multi-Charge Gun, 
gun is also approaching completion and ought to be ready for test during the coming year. Its design has been greatly modified since it was first undertaken. It is an 8-inch B. L. rifle of steel, with one principal chamber and two auxiliary chambers or pockets, designed for secondary charges and for augmenting the pressure and successively increasing the velocity of the
projectile. The original allotment for this gun was $55,000. The cost of the
forgings was $25,164. The contract price for finishing and assembling was
$28,900. Total, $54,064, leaving a balance of $936 of the allotment. January 21,
1893, Mr. J. B. Haskell, the inventor of the gun, requested permission to modify
the plan of the gun by shrinking on a band over the chamber for greater strength,
and the Board allotted a sum of $830.86 in addition to the unexpended balance
of $936, making a total of $1,766.86 for such modification. On March 14, 1893,
the inventor submitted a request for an allotment to enable him to make further
modification by shrinking on a second band, 24 inches in length, in front of the
first band and back of the trunnions, at a cost of $640.60. As the Board desired to
give the system a thorough test under favorable circumstances the request was
granted. The actual cost proved to be $5.70 more than estimated. On September
2, 1893, Mr. Haskell requested a still further allotment of $415.60 for a third band
to be shrunk on adjacent to the other two, so as to make the hooping practically
continuous from the breech to the trunnion band, making a total cost thus far of
$56,892.76.

Postponing of Haskell Multi-Charge Gun Testing due to Miscommunication on Ammunition Data-the gun is in the same condition as at the date of the last report. It is ready for
the test as soon as the projectiles and powder are obtained. An estimate of the
cost of the ammunition was received from the company, May 26, 1896, and an
allotment was made for the purchase of 20 rounds, the number fixed by the
Board for the preliminary test. The delay in testing this gun has been due entirely
to the failure of the Haskell Gun Company to furnish the necessary data in regard
to the ammunition, which was called for by the Board three years ago. It is
understood that the estimate has been delayed by the long-continued illness of
Mr. Haskell. It is expected that the gun can now be tested within a few months.

Brown gun breaks down and new one is commisioned: Of which a full account was given in the last annual report, and which had then been fired about 100 rounds, was tested to the one hundred and ninety-second round, when the gun failed, having become unserviceable…, of which a full account was given in the last annual report, and which had then been fired about 100 rounds, was tested to the one hundred and ninety-second round, when the gun failed, having become unserviceable.” 
For these reasons the Board decided to recommend the allotment of $33,000 for the construction of a 10-inch rifle, and the recommendation was approved by the Secretary of War. The Trustees of the gun report that the mill for hot rolling the segments and the machine for winding the wire on the gun with the requisite initial tension are completed and that the wire and steel for the segments have been made. The lining tube is under construction by the Bethlehem Iron Company.

description: New U.S. magazine rifle model of 1903 described: 23 aimed shots/minute single-loading; 25/minute magazine fire; muzzle velocity 2,300 ft/sec; .30 caliber; weighs ~8 lbs; magazine capacity 5 cartridges. To be issued to entire Regular Army by January 1, 1905.

Unsuccesful test: In July, 1903, the gun was returned to the proving ground and mounted for continuation of test under a program of 100 rounds approved by the Board. July 30, 1903, one shot was fired in the presence of the Board with a charge of 100 pounds of smokeless powder. The breechblock was jammed by this shot and required considerable force to open it. Firings were continued from time to time until November 1903, the breech mechanism continuing to operate unsatisfactorily. On November 4, 1903, the company requested permission, which was granted, to substitute a new breech mechanism, and the gun was removed from the proving ground in January, 1905, for the purpose. It has not yet been returned. April 7, 1904, the company requested an additional allotment of $10,000 to cover extraordinary expenses incurred in the manufacture of the gun, but the request was not approved. Under date of July 2, 1906, the company stated that there was still a balance due the Reading Iron Company for repairs to the gun and requested an allotment to cover this and the cost of a new breechblock. The Board has not approved this request, and it is doubtful when the repairs will be completed.”

General strike threatened at Government gun factory at Watervliet Arsenal. Petition sent to Gen. Crozier (Chief of Ordnance) in December 1904 requesting investigation of workers' grievances. Crozier replied investigation must be requested by post officers. Secretary Taft sustained decision. Workers appeal to President.

description: Maj. Isaac Newton Lewis redesigns the range finder at Fort Monroe. BOF arranges competitive test beginning October 7 1907 at Fort Wadsworth among Warner & Swasey; Whistler-Hearn; Lewis; Rafferty; and Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller instruments.

raw_type: Technology, Lewis Range and Position Finder
description: Competitive test of depression range and position finders at Fort Wadsworth. Over 17000 observations at ranges from 2000 to 12000 yards. Results at fixed points: Lewis 24.4 yards average error; Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller 35.1 yards; Whistler-Hearn 38.0 yards; Warner & Swasey 55.8 yards. Lewis instrument selected.
BOF repeatedly notes no action taken on Vickers-Maxim 1-pounder pending completion of McClean gun trials. Material held in abeyance.

BOF competitive test of depression position range-finders at Fort Wadsworth: 17,000 separate observations at fixed and moving targets, ranges 2,000-12,000 yards. Lewis instrument selected after supplemental test prompted by competitor protest. 29 nine-foot horizontal-base range-finders also procured.

BOF disapproves further experiments with De Knight semi-automatic machine gun; recommends non-adoption of one-pounder automatic gun. Tests in progress with 7.5-pounder and 15-pounder semi-automatic guns. BOF estimate of $100,000 cut in half by Congress; of $172,797 total fund only $73,994 available for experimental purposes.

BOF annual report (signed by Maj. Gen. Bell as president): competitive test of depression position range-finders held at Fort Wadsworth, Oct–Nov 1907 — 17,000 observations, ranges 2,000–12,000 yards; Lewis instrument selected after supplemental test. BOF also recommends against adoption of Brown segmental wire-wound gun; Crozier wire-wound gun found to have greater merit in comparative tests. BOF excuses Crozier from deliberations on wire-wound guns at his own request. BOF disapproves further experiments with De Knight semi-automatic machine gun and recommends non-adoption of one-pounder automatic gun. BOF seriously handicapped by lack of funds — $100,000 estimate cut in half by Congress.

Bell as president): competitive test of depression position range-finders held at Fort Wadsworth, Oct–Nov 1907 — 17,000 observations, ranges 2,000–12,000 yards; Lewis instrument selected after supplemental test. BOF also recommends against adoption of Brown segmental wire-wound gun; Crozier wire-wound gun found to have greater merit in comparative tests. BOF excuses Crozier from deliberations on wire-wound guns at his own request. BOF disapproves further experiments with De Knight semi-automatic machine gun and recommends non-adoption of one-pounder automatic gun. BOF seriously handicapped by lack of funds — $100,000 estimate cut in half by Congress.

Lewis substantially redesigns and upgrades range-finder model; tested at Fort Monroe and Fort Wadsworth in competitive evaluation; Lewis instrument selected as most accurate (average error 24.4 yards vs 35.1–55.8 for competitors); Crozier as Chief of Ordnance does not adopt it

BOF annual report (signed by Maj. Gen. Bell as president): competitive test of depression position range-finders held at Fort Wadsworth, Oct–Nov 1907 — 17,000 observations, ranges 2,000–12,000 yards; Lewis instrument selected after supplemental test. BOF also recommends against adoption of Brown segmental wire-wound gun; Crozier wire-wound gun found to have greater merit in comparative tests. BOF excuses Crozier from deliberations on wire-wound guns at his own request. BOF disapproves further experiments with De Knight semi-automatic machine gun and recommends non-adoption of one-pounder automatic gun. BOF seriously handicapped by lack of funds — $100,000 estimate cut in half by Congress.

Artillery Board recommends against adoption of Morris tube outfit. BOF approves recommendation and discontinues consideration. Rationale: coast-artillery service has actual targets always available; simulation unnecessary.
BOF decides not to recommend adoption of 1-pounder gun. McClean trials discontinued. No further action on Vickers-Maxim 1-pounder.

BOF tests: wireless telephone apparatus achieves conversation over 5 miles. Telephone camera proved valuable and transferred to Signal Corps. Photographic camera method for determining 'overs' and 'shorts' in artillery practice proved satisfactory; two cameras furnished to each Coast Artillery district.

BOF recommends non-adoption of Brown segmental wire-wound gun; approved by Secretary of War. In comparative tests, Crozier 6-inch wire-wound gun found to have greater merit than Brown gun. Gen. Crozier excused at his own request from all BOF deliberations on wire-wound guns. Construction of 14-inch wire-wound gun in progress.

Maj. Gen. J. F. Bell (Chief of Staff) annual report calls for Army strengthening across all branches. Bell specifically calls for 'liberal appropriation' to enable Signal Corps to continue experiments in aerial navigation.

Congress asked for $200,000 for military dirigible airships; regarded as 'hobby of visionaries' and did not appropriate. Secretary of War Wright asks for $500,000 appropriation. NY Sun notes European war departments far ahead of US in dirigible development; Signal Corps aeronautical detail of only 3 officers and 10 men.
Model 1909 Benet Mercié adopted as official light machine gun of US Army
Model 1909 Benet Mercié adopted as official light machine gun of US Army

Secretary of War Garrison issues public statement: many US seacoast defenses cannot defend themselves against land attack and were built only with sea-fire in mind. Emphasizes mobile army is essential complement to coast defenses — 'fixed defenses effective only over range of their guns.' Cites 1909 Massachusetts maneuvers where attacking troops landed at New Bedford and captured Boston from rear. Department now considering installation of 6-inch guns with all-round fire for land defense.

U.S. Army accepts Wright 1909 Military Flyer at $30000 (base contract plus speed bonus) as Signal Corps Airplane No. 1. First military airplane in U.S. service. Pilot training begins at College Park MD and Fort Sam Houston TX.
Last BOF mention: reports on Vickers-Maxim and Hotchkiss 1-pounder guns submitted to Chief of Ordnance.

Maj. Gen. J. Franklin Bell opines that heavier-than-air machines will develop as rapidly as the motor car, and that within 10 years the aeroplane will reach high state of efficiency as a military machine. Bell doubts utility of aeroplane for explosive-dropping purposes.

Second International Aviation Meet at Belmont Park, N.Y.: Grahame-White wins international speed cup (100km in 1hr 1min 4sec) in Blériot monoplane. Ralph Johnstone sets world altitude record of 9,714 ft in Wright biplane. Moisant wins Statue of Liberty race (36 miles, 34min 38sec). Army observers include Maj. Gen. Leonard Wood, Maj. Gen. Bell, Brig. Gen. Crozier, Brig. Gen. Allen and many senior officers. Navy officers note aeroplanes would pose serious threat in wartime.

Aviation tournament at Halethorpe (Baltimore): Hubert Latham flies over city of Baltimore (42 mins). Secretary of War Dickinson, Maj. Gen. Wood (Chief of Staff), Brig. Gen. Allen (Chief Signal Officer), Adm. Dewey attend. Special exhibitions of bomb throwing and rifle shooting from aeroplanes: Latham drops all 6 bombs on outlined battleship deck target from 200 ft; Drexel also hits all 6 from 150 ft. Small-arms practice from aeroplanes at 150-ft altitude. Brig. Gen. Allen announces War Dept. about to ask bids for aeroplanes capable of carrying 600 lbs of explosives.

Maj. Gen. Wood confirms Col. Lewis offered his gun to the US Government without compensation in 1910 when Wood was Chief of Staff: 'The offer was made in his presence.' Col. Lewis replies to Gen. Crozier's denial in New York Times: Lewis made offer multiple times March 1910 - September 1912 to Chief of Staff (Wood) and President of BOF, stipulating only that test not be conducted at Springfield Arsenal by Ordnance Bureau. Lewis also offered two guns free for aeroplane use in fall 1912; offer accepted by Wood and Gen. Allen but Crozier refused to issue service ammunition for unadopted gun. Lewis went to Europe in January 1913 after offers rejected.

Maj. Gen. Bell and Brig. Gen. Crozier recommend to Secretary of War that Cuba be authorized to purchase up to 10,000 U.S. Springfield rifles rather than be forced to buy European Mausers. Bell reads draft legislation authorizing sale to House Military Committee.

Col. Lewis testifies: offered Lewis gun to US Government in 1911 and again in 1912 through Chief of Staff Wood; in June 1916 offered his entire interest to Secretary Baker without compensation; none accepted. Generals Wood, Weaver, and Col. Kilbourne present when offers made. Crozier denied Lewis had ever offered gun free. Lewis offered two guns free for aeroplane maneuvers in fall 1912; Wood and Gen. Allen accepted but Crozier refused to issue service ammunition. Lewis forwarded royalty checks of ~$17,700 to War Dept.; never acknowledged; initially returned on Crozier's advice. Lewis: 'The responsibility primarily rests upon the Bureau of Ordnance... General Crozier to-day is more responsible for the obsolete and inadequate equipment that the United States forces have than any other public man.'

Secretary Stimson and Panama Fortification Board depart Washington for Isthmus (arriving July 13) to inspect canal fortification sites. Key question: distribute guns at different points vs. concentrate rifles and mortar batteries. Stimson's party includes Brig. Gen. Clarence Edwards. Separate party headed by Maj. Gen. Wood (July 10) includes Brig. Gen. Weaver (Chief of Coast Artillery), Brig. Gen. Bixby (Chief of Engineers), Cmdr. Maxwell (USN), and Capt. O'Hern (representing Ordnance Dept.).

Gen. Crozier testifies in defense of Lewis gun rejection: first offer received May 2, 1912; Springfield Arsenal test in 1913 showed Lewis gun jammed 206 times with 35 parts broken vs. Vickers gun jamming only 23 times with no parts broken. Rejects conclusions of Wood's 1916 Plattsburg test (Lewis fired by factory employees; Benét-Mercié by enlisted men). In summer 1916 Crozier on own initiative without appropriation bought 350 Lewis guns for Mexican border. Lewis gun deemed suitable for aeroplane use (per Pershing); Browning and Vickers selected for ground use. Admits 1,300 Lewis guns ordered in April 1917 anticipating tests. Royalty check returned lest Savage Arms Co. influence Ordnance Dept.; ultimately accepted by Treasury without acknowledgment.

description: On recommendation of Secretary of War Garrison, President Wilson nominates Brig. Gen. William Crozier for another four-year term as Chief of Ordnance. Crozier completes 12 years as Chief of Ordnance on Nov. 22, 1913. Nomination sent to Senate Nov. 18, reported out of Military Committee same day, confirmed immediately — Senate expedited action on learning term expired Nov. 22.

Motor tractors first considered: One of the most important pieces of work undertaken by the board this year was the provision for an exhaustive test of motor tractors for military purposes. Very considerable attention has been paid to this subject by European Governments, but in this country practically all of the development of motor tractors has been for ordinary commercial purposes, and it is time consideration was given to the application of tractors to the problems of military transportation. One of the desirable uses to which tractors could be put would be for hauling the heavy siege guns and reserve ammunition for the artillery. This board has recommended the detail of a board of officers, to include the Field Artillery, Coast Artillery, Engineers, Ordnance Department, and Quartermaster Corps, to go thoroughly into the subject and make recommendations to the Board of Ordnance and Fortifications as the basis of further action and possible allotments. In order that the board of officers may have a definite basis of comparison, an allotment of $8,000 was made on February 6, 1913, for the purchase abroad of a motor tractor of the most advanced type developed for military purposes. On June 6, 1913, this sum was further increased $1,000, and on September 16 by $1,800, to cover additional purchase price, transportation, and test. The total allotment for the purpose is therefore $10,800

Purchase of European Tractor model: Allotments aggregating $10,000 were made in 1913 for the purchase abroad of a motor tractor for military purposes. Considerable attention has been given to this subject by European Governments, but in this country practically all of the development of motor tractors has been for ordinary commercial purposes, and it was thought desirable to procure the most advanced type for test and observation in this country. Though the foreign manufacturers at first offered to sell a tractor, they finally declined to do so, but agreed to send one to the United States for test, and allotments were made on February 6 of $3,000 and on June 5, 1914, of $2,000 for transportation and all expenses of test of a Chatillon-Panhard tractor. Owing to the conditions abroad the manufacturers are now unable to furnish a tractor. All allotments have been revoked, and the subject will be taken up with manufacturers in this country.

Holt Catterpillar Company fails to sell to BOF: In 1913-14 the Holt Catterpillar Company attempted to sell these tractors to the U.S. War Department as a vehicle to carry artillery and munitions. Ben Holt wrote a letter to Senator Miles Pointdexter, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Expenditures in the War Department, trying to interest the Army in testing and potentially buying the tractors. After delay, the Senator referred the letter to Major General Leonard Wood, president of the Board of Ordnance and Fortifications, who was not interested in the invention. In 1914, Holt again wrote to the BOF, saying that his company would fully pay for a test of their model at one of the BOF’s testing grounds. He was again ignored.

Over 500 approved applications from bankers, lawyers, athletes. Instruction by Regular Army officers in Cavalry, Artillery, Signal Corps, Infantry; opportunities for work with aeroplanes and machine guns. Fort Ethan Allen concurrent machine-gun instruction camp: ~250 men from Regular Army and Organized Militia units learning Lewis gun nomenclature, range practice, reconnaissance, and pack animal management.

Armored railroad car second BOF allotment- Self-propelled armored railway car- For the development, construction, and test of a railway car propelled by a 200-horsepower oil engine with electric drive, fitted with an armored cab, searchlight, and conning tower, and carrying a normal armament of two machine guns, the sum of $74,000 was allotted February 19, 1917. The car was completed and subjected to extensive tests at Sandy Hook, N. J., which have resulted in the acquisition of valuable information concerning this type of construction. It is the intention to ship the car to Camp Humphreys, Va., where its upkeep can bo readily attended to, and where it will be valuable for the instruction of student officers in the Engineer School, An unexpended balance from this allotment of $2,072 is available for revocation. (4660)
Lewis gun production reaches 2,500 complete guns per week for Allied armies in England; French branch (Société des Armes Lewis, St. Denis) produces ~1,000 guns per week for French/Italian forces
US finally adopts Lewis gun; by May 1918, US receives 16,000 Lewis guns (10,000 for aircraft, 6,000 for Marine Corps); Savage Arms of Utica NY begins licensed production in .30-06 caliber
